glossaryForTranslators.about: Tokens written as {{token_name}} are filled in automatically at render time with real values from the Python error. You may move them to suit your language's word order, but do not translate, rename, or remove them. No HTML is needed around them as styling is applied automatically. Keys starting with _ are metadata for translators - do not translate or modify them.

name.description: The Python variable or identifier that caused the error.

loc.description: The line number and filename where the error occurred.

codeLine.description: The exact source code line that caused the error.

variants.title: Variable used before it gets a value here
variants.summary: {{name}} is used at {{loc}} before it has been given a value in the function that surrounds it. It is set later, but not before it is used.
variants.why: A variable from an enclosing function only becomes available once the line that assigns it has run. Using it earlier - for example from an inner function that runs first - leaves it without a value.
steps: Give {{name}} a value before the line that uses it.
If an inner function needs it, pass the value in as an argument instead of relying on the enclosing scope.

_placeholders.name: The enclosing-scope variable name, eg. total
_placeholders.loc: Where it was used, eg. line 3 in main.py

variants.title: This variable doesn't exist yet
variants.summary: Your code uses the variable {{name}}, but this hasn't been created yet. Check {{loc}}. If you meant to print the text {{name}}, put it in double quotes.
variants.why: Without speech marks Python treats {{name}} as a variable and this variable does not exist yet.
steps: If it is meant to be text put speech marks around {{name}}.
If it is meant to be a variable make it first (for example: {{name}} = 0).
Check spelling and capital letters.

_placeholders.name: The undefined variable name, eg. kittens
_placeholders.loc: Where it was used, eg. line 2 in main.py

variants.title: Variable used before it gets a value in this part of the code
variants.summary: Here, {{name}} is used at {{loc}} before you give it a value.
variants.why: You have used the variable before it has been given a value. If used within a subroutine, the variable must either be global and given a value outside the subroutine definition, or local and given a value inside the subroutine, before it is used.
steps: Give it a value first (add a line like {{name}} = ... before you use it).

_placeholders.name: The variable used before assignment, eg. score
_placeholders.loc: Where it was read before being set, eg. line 4 in main.py

variants.title: Missing colon (:) at the end
variants.summary: There is a colon (:) missing at the end of a line. Check {{loc}}: {{codeLine}}
variants.why: In Python constructs like if statements, for loops and while loops must have a colon at the end of their first line.
steps: Add a colon (:) at the end of that line.

_placeholders.loc: Where the missing colon is, eg. line 3 in main.py
_placeholders.codeLine: The line that needs the colon, eg. for i in range(10)

variants.title: Comma used where a colon is needed
variants.summary: This line ends with a comma, but this kind of Python line should end with a colon (:). Check {{loc}}: {{codeLine}}
variants.why: Lines that start a block (like if or for) need a colon, not a comma.
steps: Replace the comma with a colon (:).

_placeholders.loc: Where the comma is, eg. line 1 in main.py
_placeholders.codeLine: The line ending with a comma, eg. if score > 10,

variants.title: Too many colons at the end
variants.summary: This line ends with more than one colon. Check {{loc}}: {{codeLine}}
variants.why: A block-starting line needs exactly one colon at the end.
steps: Leave only one colon (:) at the end of the line.

_placeholders.loc: Where the extra colon is, eg. line 2 in main.py
_placeholders.codeLine: The line with double colons, eg. if x > 0::

variants.title: A bracket or parenthesis was not closed
variants.summary: One of your brackets, braces, or parentheses is open but never closes.
variants.why: Python needs every opening symbol like (, [ or { to have a matching closing symbol.
steps: Find the opening symbol that has no matching closing symbol.
Add the missing closing symbol.
Check nearby lines as the opening symbol may be earlier.

variants.title: Closing bracket does not match the opening one
variants.summary: A closing symbol was used, but it does not match the opening symbol.
variants.why: Each opening symbol must be closed by the same kind: () [] or {}.
steps: Find the opening symbol first.
Change the closing symbol so it matches.
Check that symbols are in the correct order.

variants.title: A string was started but not finished
variants.summary: A string opens with a quote mark but does not close.
variants.why: Text in Python must have matching opening and closing quotes.
steps: Find the string that starts but does not end.
Add the missing quote mark.
Use matching quote marks around the text.

variants.title: A comma is missing between items
variants.summary: Python expected a comma between values, but did not find one.
variants.why: Lists, dictionaries, tuples, and function arguments need commas to separate each item.
steps: Check where two values are next to each other.
Add a comma between each separate item.
Run the code again to check for the next issue.

variants.title: Assignment used instead of comparison
variants.summary: This condition uses =, but conditions must compare values. Check {{loc}}: {{codeLine}}
variants.why: In conditions, = sets a value, but == compares values.
steps: Use == to compare values in if, elif, or while conditions.
Keep = for setting a variable value outside comparisons.

_placeholders.loc: Where the = is used in a condition, eg. line 6 in main.py
_placeholders.codeLine: The condition line, eg. if score = 10:

variants.title: This operator is not valid in Python
variants.summary: This line contains ++ or --, which Python does not use.
variants.why: Python does not have increment/decrement operators like some other languages.
steps: Use += 1 to increase a value by one.
Use -= 1 to decrease a value by one.

variants.title: The line is incomplete or mismatched
variants.summary: Something is missing or extra - like a colon, bracket, or quote.
variants.why: If (), [], {}, :, or quotes don't match, Python can't read the line.
steps: Make (), [], and {} come in matching pairs.
Add the missing colon (:).
Use matching opening and closing quotes.

variants.title: The indentation isn't right here
variants.summary: Python uses the spaces at the start of a line to group code into blocks, and the spacing around {{loc}} doesn't fit. A line may be indented when it shouldn't be, or missing the indentation a block needs.
variants.why: Lines in the same block must start in the same column, a block only opens after a line ending in a colon (:), and a line can't be indented for no reason.
steps: If a line is indented but nothing above it ends in a colon (:), remove the extra spaces.
If a block was expected (after a line ending in :), indent its lines by the same amount.
Keep spacing consistent - 4 spaces per level - and don't mix tabs and spaces.

_placeholders.loc: Where the indentation problem is, eg. line 7 in main.py

variants.title: These data types don't work together
variants.summary: You're joining (concatenating) different kinds of data types. For example, a number (integer or float) and a word (string).
variants.why: Python's + means 'add' for numbers and 'join' for text, so both sides have to be the same kind. It can't add a number to a piece of text.
steps: To join them as text, convert the number with str(...).
To add them as numbers instead, make sure neither side is text.

variants.title: Lists don't have that feature
variants.summary: You're calling a list method that doesn't exist.
variants.why: Lists use .append(value) or .extend([...]). There is no .push().
steps: Use list.append(one_item).
Use list.extend([many, items]).

variants.title: That name after the dot isn't available for this value
variants.summary: At {{loc}}, {{codeLine}} uses a name after the dot that this kind of value doesn't have. It might be a typo, or a method that belongs to a different type of value.
variants.why: After a dot, Python looks for a method or property that belongs to that exact kind of value - a string, a list, a number, and so on. If that kind of value has nothing by that name, you get this error.
steps: Check the spelling and capital letters of the name after the dot.
Make sure the value is the kind you expect - for example a list has .append(), but a string does not.
Look up which methods that kind of value supports, and use one of those.

_placeholders.loc: Where the attribute was used, eg. line 2 in main.py
_placeholders.codeLine: The line with the dotted access, eg. name.shrink()

variants.title: This index position does not exist
variants.summary: You are trying to use an index position that does not exist. The list is not that long.
variants.why: Items are counted from 0. In myList = ["A", "B", "C"], "A" is at index 0, "B" at 1 and "C" at 2 - so the largest valid index is 2, and index 3 does not exist.
steps: Check the length with len(the_list).
Use an index position number smaller than len(the_list).

variants.title: That key isn't in the dictionary
variants.summary: You looked up a key that the dictionary doesn't have.
variants.why: The dictionary has no value for that key.
steps: Check the exact key (spelling and capital letters).
It might be missing, use dictionary.get(key, default) or add the key.

variants.title: You can't divide by zero
variants.summary: You are trying to divide a number by zero, which is not possible.
variants.why: In mathematics, division by zero is undefined.
steps: Change the calculation so that you are not dividing a number by 0.
